case_name: Little Shuswap Lake Indian Band v. August-Sjodin
text:
2020 BCSC 607 Little Shuswap Lake Indian Band v. August-Sjodin
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF BRITISH COLUMBIA
Citation:
Little Shuswap Lake Indian Band v. August-Sjodin,
2020 BCSC 607
Date: 20200421
Docket: 52560
Registry: Kamloops
Between:
Little Shuswap Lake Indian Band
Plaintiff
And
Peter Allan August-Sjodin
Defendant
Before: The Honourable Madam Justice Donegan
Reasons for Judgment on Contempt Application
Counsel for the Plaintiff:
A.M. Grant
Appearing on his own behalf:
P.A. August-Sjodin
Place and Date of Hearing:
Kamloops, B.C.
March 13, 2020
Place and Date of Judgment:
Kamloops, B.C.
April 21, 2020
INTRODUCTION
[1] On July 4, 2016, Justice Hyslop permanently restrained Peter August-Sjodin from entering the Quaaout Resort and Conference Centre, located at 1663 Little Shuswap Lake Road, Chase, British Columbia (the "Quaaout Resort and Conference Centre"). The Little Shuswap Lake Indian Band (the "Band") alleges that he failed to comply with this order on two occasions and now seeks to have him found in contempt of court.
BACKGROUND
[2] In support of its application, the Band tendered the following evidence:
1. The affidavit of Kukpi'7 (or, in English, "Chief") Oliver Arnouse sworn December 21, 2019;
2. The affidavit of Thomas Rhys Laug, Operations Manager of the Quaaout Resort and Conference Centre sworn January 23, 2017; and
3. The affidavit of Rheanna Arnouse, an employee of the Band, sworn on January 26, 2017.
[3] Mr. August-Sjodin does not challenge the credibility of the affiants or the reliability of their evidence. I find them all credible and their evidence reliable. Before turning to the details of their evidence, I wish to first identify and explain two features of the evidence.
[4] First, the affiants occasionally refer to information they were given from others. I appreciate this information is not offered for its truth, but simply to explain or provide context for what the affiants said or did. I am careful to rely upon this type of evidence for only the limited, non-hearsay purpose for which it was tendered.
[5] Second, the affidavit of Kukpi'7 Arnouse contains evidence that is irrelevant to this application. I heard two other applications, from separate proceedings involving the same parties, on the same day as the within application. Kukpi'7 Arnouse has relevant evidence to give in all three applications and, for reasons of convenience, quite reasonably tendered one affidavit covering all matters. I am careful not to consider any evidence that is either irrelevant to the within application or for which its prejudicial effect outweighs its probative value.
[6] Mr. August-Sjodin is a member of the Band. In recent years he has identified using other names, such as ":spi7uwe" or ":spi7uwe./:secwepemc", or some other such similar name.
[7] On January 28, 2015, the Band took steps to ban Mr. August-Sjodin from various Band facilities, including the Quaaout Resort and Conference Centre. On February 18, 2016, the Band commenced this proceeding, seeking to have the ban enforced, to have him restrained from having contact with certain Band members and employees, and to have him restrained from issuing documents that he called "true bills" (lengthy, often nonsensical documents, covered in ink thumbprints and other markings that purport to be some kind of notice or statement of account).
[8] Mr. August-Sjodin filed a Response and the Band soon thereafter brought applications to strike his Response, obtain default judgment and various other orders in the nature of injunctive relief.
[9] Justice Hyslop heard the Band's applications on May 10, 2016. On July 4, 2016, in reasons indexed at 2016 BCSC 1214, the court ordered the following:
[45] I order the following:
1. The defendant, Mr. August-Sjodin, is restrained from entering any of the following buildings or locations at any time:
a) Little Shuswap Lake Indian Band Administration offices, located at 1886 Little Shuswap Lake Road, Chase, British Columbia;
b) Skwlax Wellness Centre, located at #15 - 1528 Little Shuswap Lake Road, Chase, British Columbia;
c) Little Shuswap Lake Gas Station, located at 440 Squilax-Anglemont Road, Chase, British Columbia;
d) Quaaout Resort and Conference Centre, located at 1663 Little Shuswap Lake Road, Chase, British Columbia;
e) Talking Rock Golf Course, located at 1663 Little Shuswap Lake Road, Chase, British Columbia;
f) Skwlax Daycare, located at 1663 Little Shuswap Lake Road, Chase, British Columbia;
2. The defendant, Mr. August-Sjodin, is restrained from initiating, directly or indirectly, any written, verbal, or physical contact with any of the following people at any time:
a) Kathy Thibeault;
b) Desira John;
c) Kristina John;
d) H. Jane Wakefield;
e) Amy Dennis;
f) Teresa Tomma;
g) Stephen Wittmer;
h) Rhys Laug;
i) Jeff Lee;
j) Felix Arnouse;
k) Oliver Arnouse;
l) Brian Finlay;
m) Wes Francois; and
n) Dale Tomma
3. The defendant, Mr. August-Sjodin, is restrained from initiating, directly or indirectly, any written, verbal, or physical contact with any of the Band's employees, officials, agents or contractors, except by written communications addressed to:
Band Administrator
Little Shuswap Lake Indian Band
1886 Little Shuswap Lake Road
Chase, British Columbia V0E 1M2
Fax 250-679-3220
4. The defendant, Mr. August-Sjodin, is retrained from issuing to any person any further document (including invoices, notices or "true bills") alleging breach of trademark respecting use of his name, without prior leave of this Honourable Court; and
5. Costs.
[10] With respect to costs, the court ordered they be assessed as special costs.
[11] In making these orders, Justice Hyslop found Mr. August-Sjodin and his pleadings fell within a particular category of vexatious litigants identified by Justice Rooke in Meads v. Meads, 2012 ABQB 571 as "Organized Pseudolegal Commercial Argument Litigant" ("OPCA Litigants"), sometimes called "freeman on the land". In striking his Amended Response to Civil Claim, Justice Hyslop found his pleadings were incoherent, unresponsive, disclosed no reasonable defence, were an abuse of the court process, scandalous, frivolous and vexatious.
[12] Immediately before detailing the orders, Justice Hyslop warned Mr. August-Sjodin about the possible consequences of his non-compliance with them. She wrote:
[44] Mr. August-Sjodin simply does not care what damages he causes his Band. It is apparent that without an injunction, his behaviour will continue and the damage to the Band will continue. By granting a permanent injunction, it reduces and prevents harm to the Band. Once communicated to Mr. August-Sjodin, if any of the terms of an injunction are breached, then it is open to the Band to pursue contempt proceedings against Mr. August-Sjodin.
[13] Thomas Rhys Laug has been an employee of Skwlax Investments Inc., a corporation owned and controlled by the Band. He has worked at the Quaaout Resort and Conference Centre since 2010 and in March 2015 became its Operations Manager, responsible for general day-to-day oversight.
[14] Mr. Laug has known Mr. August-Sjodin for a number of years. He explained that the Band is not a huge community and over the years he has come to know many of its members who live on or near the Band's Reserves, including Mr. August- Sjodin. Mr. Laug described some of his interactions with Mr. August-Sjodin, including his unusual demands and behaviour over the last several years. He noticed that Mr. August-Sjodin started using different names, such as ":spi7uwe./:secwepemc" or ":spi7uwe".
[15] When Mr. Laug learned of Justice Hyslop's orders, he issued a written memorandum on July 18, 2016 to all Quaaout Resort and Conference Centre staff, advising them of the orders.
[16] On December 22, 2016 at 5:14 p.m., Mr. Laug was working inside the Quaaout Resort and Conference Centre when he noticed a person he thought was Mr. August-Sjodin inside the premises. The person was with another person Mr. Laug recognized - Vern August. Mr. Laug knows Vern August to be Mr. August-Sjodin's uncle. At 6:44 p.m., Mr. Laug confirmed that Mr. August-Sjodin was indeed inside the Quaaout Resort and Conference Centre when he saw Mr. August-Sjodin walk through the Quaaout Resort and Conference Centre lobby and exit though the front doors. As he walked out the front doors, Mr. Laug noticed that Vern August was speaking with a front desk agent. Mr. Laug believed, from what others had told him, that the RCMP had been called.
[17] When Mr. Laug observed Mr. August-Sjodin walk out the front doors of the Quaaout Resort and Conference Centre, he followed him out and the two men had a conversation outside the entrance. Mr. Laug told him that they were under instructions to call the RCMP if he came to the Quaaout Resort and Conference Centre. Mr. August-Sjodin replied that the Band and RCMP did not have jurisdiction "over these lands" or words to that effect.
[18] Mr. Laug then recorded what occurred in writing and took steps to secure video camera footage from three cameras in and about the Quaaout Resort and Conference Centre from that day. The cameras were located outside the entrance, over the front desk and overlooking the pool and hot tub area. No issue is raised with respect to their authenticity. Mr. Laug later reviewed this video camera footage and identified Mr. August-Sjodin as a person appearing in the hot tub and pool areas that day.
[19] As a result of information from another staff member working at the front desk, Mr. Laug also retrieved security camera footage from the same three cameras from two days earlier, December 20, 2016. Mr. Laug reviewed that security footage as well and identified Mr. August-Sjodin at the front desk at 5:00 p.m. and in the pool area at 6:26 p.m.
[20] Mr. Laug also took steps to retrieve the Quaaout Resort and Conference Centre's pool and fitness facility waiver forms for December 20 and 22, 2016. He identified a signature on the form for each of those days that he recognized as one of the names used by Mr. August-Sjodin. From his previous correspondence and familiarity with Mr. August-Sjodin, Mr. Laug is familiar with the other names Mr. August-Sjodin uses. The name written on the two forms is the same - ":spi7uwe:ucc1-207". He recognized this as one of the names used by Mr. August-Sjodin.
[21] Mr. Laug's affidavit was sworn on January 23, 2017, but no steps were taken by the Band to pursue any remedy until now. Kukpi'7 Arnouse explained why. He deposes, and I accept:
36. By the middle of 2017, the Band Council was hopeful that Peter August-Sjodin had ceased pursuing the "True Bill" and other such "freeman-on-the-land" activities. As a result, we had held off on taking any proceedings in seeking contempt or enforcement of the Order of July 4, 2016. I personally thought that Peter August-Sjodin was generally acting in accordance with the Order and that the December 20 and 22, 2016 breaches of the Order were relatively minor. As we have more recently discovered, the Band Council's hope was misplaced: Peter August-Sjodin had not ceased his "freeman-on-the-land" activities.
[22] The latter portion of this paragraph is a reference to other matters before the court in a separate application, but provides a reasonable explanation for the Band's delay in pursuing the within application.
[23] Mr. August-Sjodin elected not to file a response or call any evidence on this application, but I did permit him to file a letter addressed to the Ministry of the Attorney General and make oral submissions. I note that he signed his letter to the Attorney General with "ucc1-207:spi7uwe", as well as ":spi7uwe".
[24] The defendant's submissions were not responsive to the application, but he was clear that he does not feel the court has jurisdiction over him.
Legal Principles
[25] Contempt proceedings initiated by a party are governed by the Supreme Court Civil Rules, B.C. Reg. 168/2009 [Civil Rules]. Rules 22-8(11) and (12) codify common-law requirements for notice and service. They provide:
Proceeding for contempt
(11) A party applying for an order for contempt must serve the alleged contemnor with a copy of the filed notice of application and all filed affidavits in support of it at least 7 days before the hearing of the application.
Affidavit
(12) An application under subrule (11) must be supported by affidavit setting out the conduct alleged to be contempt of court.
[26] Strict compliance with Rule 22-8 is necessary. As I indicated at the hearing, I am satisfied the applicant has complied with these notice and service requirements.
[27] Civil contempt generally involves private injury or wrong through the disobedience of judgments, court orders or other court processes. Nevertheless, civil contempt is quasi-criminal in nature: Carey v. Laiken, 2015 SCC 17 at para. 42. Persons accused of contempt are presumed innocent until proven guilty beyond a reasonable doubt.
[28] Civil contempt has three elements. Each element must be proven beyond a reasonable doubt. They are:
1. the existence of a court order that clearly and unequivocally says what should and should not be done;
2. actual knowledge of the court order by the alleged contemnor; and
3. the intentional committing of an act that the order prohibits or the intentional omission of an act that the order compels
Carey at paras. 32-35.
[29] The actus reus for civil contempt is an intentional breach of a court order: MacMillan Bloedel Ltd. v. Brown, [1994] B.C.J. No. 268 (CA) at para. 91. The required intention "relates to the act itself, not to the disobedience": TG Industries Ltd. v. Williams, 2001 NSCA 105 at para. 17.
[30] The mens rea for civil contempt is simply the intention to do the act which is in fact prohibited by the order. The necessary mens rea for civil contempt resulting from a breach of court order is wilful, rather than accidental, disobedience of the court order. It is not necessary to prove direct intention to disobey the order, though it is necessary to prove the contemnor was aware of the order: North Vancouver (District) v. Sorrenti, 2004 BCCA 316 at paras. 11-12.
[31] A person cannot be convicted of contempt for breach of a court order if he did not know of the existence of the order or its terms. Actual knowledge of the order and its terms must be proven beyond a reasonable doubt: Pintea v. Johns, 2017 SCC 23. In this regard, Rule 22-8(14) of the Civil Rules provides that service of an order is not required, as long as there was actual notice. Rule 22-8(14) provides:
Service of order not necessary
(14) If the court is satisfied that a person has actual notice of the terms of an order of the court, it may find the person guilty of contempt for disobedience of the order, even though the order has not been served on the person.
[32] Because these proceedings are quasi-criminal in nature, the rules of strictissimi juris apply. The proceedings require strict adherence to rules of evidence, a precision in pleadings, procedure and evidence: Peel Financial Holdings Ltd. v. Western Delta Lands Partnership, 2003 BCCA 551 at para. 18 and Bassette v. Maggee, 2015 BCCA 422 at para. 35.
Decision
Liability for Contempt
[33] I have no hesitation in finding that the Band has proven beyond a reasonable doubt that Mr. August-Sjodin committed civil contempt of court on both December 20 and 22, 2016.
[34] The Band has proven the existence of a court order that clearly and unequivocally says what Mr. August-Sjodin should not do. The terms of Justice Hyslop's July 4, 2016 orders could not have been clearer. In particular, the order pertaining to the Quaaout Resort and Conference Centre (order 1(d)) was clear and unequivocal in restraining Mr. August-Sjodin from entering the Quaaout Resort and Conference Centre, located at 1663 Little Shuswap Lake Road, Chase, British Columbia.
[35] The Band has proven that Mr. August-Sjodin had actual knowledge of this court order. Justice Hyslop's written Reasons for Judgment were sent to Mr. August-Sjodin. When Mr. Laug spoke to him after he left the Quaaout Resort and Conference Centre on December 22, 2016, Mr. August-Sjodin made it clear he was aware of the above order. His response to Mr. Laug is consistent with his submissions before the court on this application - that he is aware of the order, but simply feels the court has no jurisdiction over him in this matter.
[36] The Band has also proven that Mr. August-Sjodin entered the Quaaout Resort and Conference Centre on December 20 and 22, 2016. Mr. Laug knows him well and saw him inside and spoke with him right after he exited the front entrance doors on December 22, 2016. He confirmed Mr. August-Sjodin's presence there that day from the video surveillance recordings. Mr. Laug also confirmed Mr. August-Sjodin's presence inside the Quaaout Resort and Conference Centre on December 20, 2016 through the video surveillance recordings. Not only did Mr. August-Sjodin enter the Quaaout Resort and Conference Centre on both of these days in contravention of the order, he remained inside for extended periods of time and used the pool.
[37] Mr. August-Sjodin did not attempt to hide his presence inside the Quaaout Resort and Conference Centre on either occasion. In fact, he signed the pool's waiver form on both occasions, using one of his unique names. There is simply no question that the Band has proven that Mr. August-Sjodin intentionally committed an act, on both December 20 and December 22, 2016, that the order prohibits. He not only wilfully breached the order, he did so openly and brazenly. He offers no lawful excuse for doing so. I find him in contempt of court on both occasions.
Costs
[38] The applicant seeks special costs of this application, to be assessed. The circumstances here justify such an award. Mr. August-Sjodin wilfully and flagrantly disobeyed a court order on two occasions. He is unapologetic and remains defiant that he is not bound to comply. The Band, having obtained a court order, is entitled to have it obeyed without having to incur any further expense. That this application became necessary represents yet another example of Mr. August-Sjodin's complete lack of concern about the damage he does to the Band and his fellow Band members. His conduct is reprehensible.
Penalty
[39] As a general rule, civil contempt proceedings are bifurcated into a liability phase and then, if liability is proven, a penalty phase: Carey at para. 18. Having now found Mr. August-Sjodin in contempt of Justice Hyslop's July 4, 2016 order, I order Mr. August-Sjodin to attend court next on Monday, June 1, 2020 at 10 a.m., by telephone, to fix a date for a hearing to determine the penalty to be imposed. Counsel for the applicant should attend the fix date by telephone as well.
"S.A. Donegan J."
DONEGAN J.
